title: Wireshark TLS Decryption
description: Decrypting TLS traffic in Wireshark using session keys or RSA private keys.
fullExplanation: Wireshark can decrypt TLS traffic if you provide the session keys. The most reliable method is the SSLKEYLOGFILE — browsers (Chrome, Firefox) can log TLS session keys to a file when this environment variable is set. Wireshark reads this file and decrypts the traffic. For older TLS 1.2 with RSA key exchange (no forward secrecy), you can provide the server's RSA private key. TLS 1.3 and TLS 1.2 with ECDHE cannot be decrypted with just the private key — you need the session keys.

steps: Launch browser from terminal (inherits env var)
Browse to target site — keys logged automatically
In Wireshark: Edit → Preferences → Protocols → TLS → (Pre)-Master-Secret log filename → select keys.log
Wireshark decrypts all captured TLS sessions
methods.works_with: TLS 1.2 and 1.3, any cipher suite including ECDHE

steps: Obtain server's RSA private key (only works if you own the server)
In Wireshark: Edit → Preferences → Protocols → TLS → RSA Keys → Add
Enter IP, port, protocol, and path to private key file
methods.works_with: TLS 1.2 with RSA key exchange ONLY. Does NOT work with ECDHE (forward secrecy).

keyFacts: SSLKEYLOGFILE works with Chrome, Firefox, curl, and many other TLS clients
TLS 1.3 always uses ECDHE — RSA private key method never works
Forward secrecy: even with private key, past sessions cannot be decrypted
SSLKEYLOGFILE is a security risk — protect the key log file carefully
Wireshark shows decrypted data in 'Decrypted TLS' tab in packet details
Follow TLS Stream: right-click → Follow → TLS Stream — see plaintext HTTP
Corporate proxies: SSL inspection uses MITM — they have session keys
